items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.q: What three notes form a major triad in root position?
items.why: Students think 'three notes = a triad'. Triads are specific: stacked thirds from the root. C major = C, E, G (skipping D and F). Consecutive scale notes are clusters, not triads.

items.correct: A recurring main theme (A) alternates with contrasting episodes (B, C, etc.).
items.misconception: A round where voices enter at different times.
items.why: Students confuse rondo (alternating refrain) with round (canon). Rondo: ABACA, ABACABA, etc. The A theme keeps coming back, like a refrain.

items.q: Middle C sits where on treble stave?
items.why: Correct answer: One ledger line below. “Top line” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The home note of a key is called.
items.why: It's Tonic. “Octave” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many notes in a C major scale (including the upper C)?
items.why: Exact answer: 8. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
